title: What's Next?
intro: Your task manager is live, working, and growing! Think of your V1 like a seed in the ground — it needs sunlight, water, and pruning to become a tree. Now it's time to plan what comes next. Make smart decisions about your app's future.

teach.title: You Built Something Real
teach.body: Congratulations — you went from a blank screen to a deployed, working app. You planned features, chose a stack, built a UI, designed a database, added auth, wrote tests, and shipped to production. That's the full software development lifecycle.

teach.title: V1 Is Like Planting a Seed
teach.body: Your first version is a seed in the ground, not the finished tree. You've done the hard work of planting it — now it needs sunlight (users), water (updates), and pruning (removing what doesn't work). The biggest mistake is digging it up every week to try a different seed.

teach.title: Running a Restaurant After Opening Night
teach.body: Launching your V1 is like a restaurant's opening night. You planned the menu, decorated the space, and trained the staff. But now real diners walk in — some love the pasta but ignore the salad, some ask for dishes you never considered. You don't throw out the whole menu; you adjust based on what people actually order. Product iteration works the same way: keep what works, cut what doesn't, and add what customers keep asking for.

teach.title: What Comes After V1?
teach.body: Now that your MVP is live, you'll get real user feedback. Some features you thought were essential will go unused. Some things you didn't think of will be requested constantly. Listen to your users, but filter through your product vision.

You now have every skill needed to build and ship a real product with AI. The next step is iterating based on user feedback and adding a payment flow — you're one Stripe integration away from your first dollar.
V1 is just the beginning. Use real user data and feedback to guide what you build next. Resist the urge to add everything — stay focused on what matters most.

start.narrative: Your task manager has been live for two weeks. You have 200 active users and a backlog of feedback. Where do you focus next?

choices.text: Build every feature users requested — team sharing, AI summaries, calendar sync, mobile app
Look at the data: which features were requested most? Which users churned? Pick the top 2-3 to build.
Rewrite the app from scratch with a better architecture now that you know what users want

overwhelmed.narrative: You try to build everything at once. After a month, nothing is finished. Half-built features create bugs, and users are confused by an inconsistent experience. What do you do?

choices.text: Stop, remove half-finished features, and pick the top 2 most-requested ones to finish properly
Push through and try to finish everything

focused.narrative: The data is clear: 80% of users want due-date reminders, and 60% complain about the mobile experience. You're ready to commit to a list.

choices.text: Pick due-date reminders and mobile improvements as the next two bets

rewrite-trap.narrative: You spend 6 weeks rewriting. The new version has fewer features than the old one. Users leave because nothing new has shipped. You've fallen into the 'rewrite trap.'

choices.text: Stop the rewrite, go back to the working app, and use the real feedback you collected to pick 2 high-impact improvements
Push through the rewrite — sunk-cost is sunk-cost, you'll finish it eventually

scope.narrative: You have 2 candidate features. Before you start building, how do you scope them?

choices.text: Define the smallest useful version of each — what's the one screen, one query, one notification that delivers the value?
Spec out the complete feature with every edge case — settings page, custom recurrences, snooze options, the works
Skip scoping — start coding and see where you end up

ship.narrative: Reminders are scoped down to a single email at 9am the day a task is due. Time to ship. How do you roll it out?

choices.text: Hide it behind a feature flag, roll out to 10% of users first, watch the metrics, then expand
Ship it to 100% of users immediately so everyone gets it at once
Push to production without any tests or monitoring — you'll fix issues if users complain

measure.narrative: Reminders are out. A week later you have data: open rates, click-through, opt-outs, and a small bump in retention. How do you decide what to build next?

choices.text: Compare the bump against your hypothesis. Did reminders move the needle? Use that signal to decide whether to invest more in reminders or pivot to mobile.
Skip the analysis — you feel good about reminders, just keep adding to them
Ignore the metrics and start a new feature you personally find more exciting

excellent-end.narrative: Three months in, you ship 2-3 small, measured improvements every month. Each one is informed by real user data, scoped to the smallest useful version, rolled out gradually, and evaluated against a clear hypothesis. You have 1,000 active users, a roadmap grounded in evidence, and a sustainable pace.
excellent-end.feedback: This is how durable products get built — analyze data, pick a small bet, scope it down, ship it safely, measure honestly, repeat. You stayed focused, listened to users, and let evidence (not enthusiasm) drive the roadmap. Congratulations on completing the Task Manager project!

mediocre-end.narrative: Reminders keep growing — recurring schedules, custom times, snooze, push notifications. Some users love it, but the original 80% who only wanted a simple email are buried under settings they don't need. Other priorities slip.
mediocre-end.feedback: Doubling down on a feature without measuring its actual impact leads to over-engineering. Always tie investment to evidence: did the last increment move retention, or just feel productive?

weak-end.narrative: You ignore the reminders data and chase a new shiny feature. Users notice you're not improving the things they actually use. Retention flatlines and the next batch of feedback is more critical than ever.
weak-end.feedback: Building what excites you instead of what users need is how side projects die. The data is the whole point of shipping — if you don't read it, you might as well have stayed in planning. Let evidence drive the next bet.
